This implementation is very similar to the HuggingFace's. 

For HuggingFace:
* Mixin defines the special tokens, and the structure enforced on the input: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/tokenization_utils_base.py#L896
* `PreTrainedTokenizer` keeps a separate encoder map for special tokens that are added later: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/tokenization_utils.py#L430 (also creates a trie to save all those tokens that are not to be split: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/tokenization_utils.py#L445)
* Added tokens encoder map is checked first when doing encoding: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/tokenization_utils.py#L586
* Regex based approach is used to distinguish between non-special and special tokens: https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/3f936df66287f557c6528912a9a68d7850913b9b/src/transformers/tokenization_utils.py#L511

Of course, HuggingFace's implementation is more generic and is extensible to all of their tokenizers through inheritance. `GPT2Tokenizer` inherits `PreTrainedTokenizer` which inherits `PreTrainedTokenizerBase`, which implements two mixins: SpecialTokensMixin, PushToHubMixin. Comparatively torchtext's tokenizers all inherit `nn.Module` with their respective (and fairly separated imo) C++ implementation.

This pull request is the minimal implementation of their general Python approach. That's why I kept the Python API to be the same. A more general implementation is possible as C++ supports multiple inheritance. But it might take a bit more time and require a broader discussion with your team.

I'm using this branch for a project and found this. This block doesn't make sense and also doesn't work.

`get_type_hints` gets a mapping from field name to field type for the dataclass `value`. You're iterating over that, but you only ever check the first item because both branches end with `return`. I'm pretty sure both of those returns in the loop should only be returning if the value is False.

On the first branch, if `field_type` is a TypeVar, you check the field value against all the arguments for the subscripted generic `type_`. Why is `get_args` in the loop? Why is `getattr` in the inner loop? Neither one is changing at that point. I believe this would also fail to detect an incorrect type if, say, a field is hinted `AnyStr`, the value is None, and the dataclass has two type arguments, one `str` and one `None`.

I assume the second branch is trying to check if the *field* value matches the *field* type? But what you're actually doing is checking if the *dataclass* matches the *subscripted dataclass type*. This crashes with the exception `TypeError: Subscripted generics cannot be used with class and instance checks` for obvious reasons.

For the false negative issue with multiple type arguments, possibly something could be done with `__orig_bases__`? Not sure.

Here's my suggestion for a fixed version. I haven't written test cases, but I did try it with my fairly complicated use case and it seems to work.
```suggestion
    type_args = get_args(type_)
    type_hints = cache(get_type_hints)(type(value))
    for field_name, field_type in type_hints.items():
        field_value = getattr(value, field_name, None)
        if isinstance(field_type, TypeVar):
            # TODO: this will fail to detect incorrect type in some cases
            # see comments on https://github.com/konradhalas/dacite/pull/209
            if not any(is_instance(field_value, arg) for arg in type_args):
                return False
        elif get_origin(field_type) is not ClassVar:
            if not is_instance(field_value, field_type):
                return False
```

I think there's a simpler solution here.

When we schedule a task: https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/core/tasks/scheduler.py#L143 we are able to pass in arbitrary args and kwargs for the job constructor.

If we update the job constructor to allow a supplied job_id to be passed in:
https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/develop/kolibri/core/tasks/job.py#L90:
`self.job_id = kwargs.get("job_id", uuid.uuid4().hex)`

Then we can just choose a constant job_id for the scheduled pingback task and just check that it exists.

This also has the virtue that we can schedule additional tasks for the `_ping` if we want.

I guess it can work by patching `_live_receivers()` and instead of returning an array of receivers (=functions) we wrap all the elements of the array in a function that creates a span and then calls the old receiver.

Something like this:
```python
old_live_receivers = Signal._live_receivers

def _sentry_live_receivers(self, sender)
    receivers = old_live_receivers(self, sender)

    def _receiver_with_span(*args, **kwargs):
        old_receiver = receivers[idx]
        name = old_receiver.__name__
        with hub.start_span(    
            op="django.signals",
            description=name,
        ) as span:
            span.set_data("signal", name)
            return old_receiver(*args, **kwargs)
            
    for idx, receiver in enumerate(receivers):
        receivers[idx] = _receiver_with_span

    return receivers

Signal._live_receivers = _sentry_live_receivers
```

OK, now that the wonky multiple-process-call behavior is fixed, I'll implement this via environment variable `OCRD_PROFILE` and `OCRD_PROFILE_FILE`

* `OCRD_PROFILE` not set or empty: no profiling
* `OCRD_PROFILE_FILE` set to a value (== current `--profile-file` flag):
  * if `OCRD_PROFILE` is defined but empty: no profiling
  * if `OCRD_PROFILE` is not defined: set `OCRD_PROFILE` to `CPU`
  * if `OCRD_PROFILE` is defined but does not contain `CPU`: raise error
* `OCRD_PROFILE` contains `CPU`, do CPU profiling (== current `--profile` flag)
* `OCRD_PROFILE` contains `RSS` do RSS memory profiling
* `OCRD_PROFILE` contains `PSS`: do proportionate mem profiling
* If `OCRD_PROFILE` contains both `PSS` and `RSS`: raise error

I don't think this is the right solution. Even when the price is empty on a stockrecord, the product could still have a price through the strategy.

The purchaseinfo "object" has a price, availability and a stockrecord
https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/partner/strategy.py#L14

The price is any of those (assuming a project has no custom ones)
https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/partner/prices.py

So whenever a product has no price (even through the strategy) it will have the Unavailable price object
https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/partner/prices.py#L40

So I see two options

1. Change the template, to make sure it checks whether a price exists
https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/templates/oscar/catalogue/partials/add_to_basket_form.html#L8
... and session.price.exists

2. Pass the price as argument in this method
https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/partner/availability.py#L36

Which you would have to do here: https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/master/src/oscar/apps/partner/strategy.py#L126

And check session.price.exists here instead.

I think solution 2 would be cleaner, as it doesn't require a template change but it does require a change in the strategy.

Here, I think, we would be forcing a default value for `OT_LOG_OUTPUT` and therefore for `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_OUTPUT` config  from the CMake makefile itself. 

We don't do the same for other CMake options, for example we have `OT_COMMISSIONER` CMake option where if user include we enable commissioner and define `"OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_COMMISSIONER_ENABLE=1"`. If user does not use `OT_COMMISSIONER` we don't define `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_COMMISSIONER_ENABLE` in CMake which let it be defined by other headers (e.g., from a project specific config header through `OT_CONFIG`). And the default value for all configs options is provided in `src/core/config` modules.

I suggest we do the same for `LOG_OUTPUT` as well (to remain consistent on how CMake options work) and also allow project specific config header to define/change `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_LOG_OUTPUT`.

`assert` is not intended to be an "elegant" shorthand for raising an exception, it's a way of programmatically making assertions about how your code should work. You use it for things like enforcing a function's contract or documenting assumptions made by the programmer (which are then validated at runtime).

In this specific case, you cannot use it because it *will not work*. Assertions are to be treated as *optional* statements and if you run Python in optimized mode, they will simply not run at all, for example:

```bash
$ python -Oc "assert False"
$ python -c "assert False"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
AssertionError
```

it wasn't clear on my first read, but the interface for the `insert` functions is pretty difficult to use -- 

The `insert` takes a string `value`, but it doesn't actually store the value -- instead it opaquely hashes it and stores that hash (which `get` would return, _not_ the value itself). This interface would be impossible to use for a caller who is managing side storage for large values themselves (which is probably the best approach -- managing the item storage seems like an orthogonal concern to the marf index).

I think an interface like:

```rust
insert(&mut self, key: &[u8; 32], value: &[u8; 40]) -> Result<(), Error>;
get(&mut self, block_hash: &BlockHeaderHash, key: &[u8; 32]) -> Result<Option<MARFValue>, Error>;
```
Would be more ergonomic.

The idea is then you can service each work queue with a separate thread.

Okay, I’ve made `dataclass_compat` mutate when possible (which is
currently always). The code path where we need to copy can be tested by
applying this patch:

```diff
diff --git a/tensorboard/dataclass_compat.py b/tensorboard/dataclass_compat.py
index eb57af0a..51d2063d 100644
--- a/tensorboard/dataclass_compat.py
+++ b/tensorboard/dataclass_compat.py
@@ -95,4 +95,6 @@ def _migrate_value(value):
 def _migrate_scalar_value(value):
-    value.metadata.data_class = summary_pb2.DATA_CLASS_SCALAR
-    return (value,)
+    new_value = summary_pb2.Summary.Value()
+    new_value.CopyFrom(value)
+    new_value.metadata.data_class = summary_pb2.DATA_CLASS_SCALAR
+    return (new_value,)
```

…and checking that all tests still pass. (They do.)

For what it’s worth, I looked into making `data_compat` mutate, too, and
concluded that it would be safe to do so for all the current call sites,
but that the savings were less obvious because `data_compat` creates
structurally different events rather than just changing a single field.

I think you're missing some important context around how these `n_features_in_` and `best_iteration_` attributes work.

As you can see in the methods, before returning a value the function `self.__sklearn_is_fitted__()` is called. That method returns `True` if `self.fitted_` exists and is `True` (see #4636).

https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/blob/29796eee6eabb79e161880976ec02fcd95dfcc4a/python-package/lightgbm/sklearn.py#L548-L549

If `self.__sklearn_is_fitted__()` returns `True`, the attributes referenced by `self.n_features_in_` and `self.best_iteration_` should not be `None`. Fitting a model should set all of those attributes.

But `mypy` can't possibly know about those specifics of how `lightgbm` is expected to work and be used, and it's rightly complaining that "hey, you initialized `self.best_iteration` to `None` and now you're returning it...therefore it's possible for that value to be `None`".

I don't want to change these type hints to `Optional[None]` because then any other code relying on these attributes (including code in projects downstream of `lightgbm` like `optuna`) will have to face this awkwardness and either use `# type: ignore` or add defensive code checking like `if model.best_iteration is not None: ...`.

I tried one approach to work around these back in 2021 (https://github.com/microsoft/LightGBM/pull/4837#discussion_r759757551) that we decided to not adopt, and looking back now I agree that adding assertions or other new complexity might not be the best way to handle this.

I think a better approach would be to initialize those attributes to values of the expected return type, e.g. to initialize `self.best_score_` to `-1.0`.

But I think that needs to be done by someone who's more familiar with LightGBM, like one of the maintainers here, because it's important to, for example, check for cases where those internal attributes are being compared to `None`.

We're having two types of caches happening here.

- `token` + `token_updater` is the current token cache plus a callback to store the cache when it has changed.
- `cache_file` is a path to a file that data is written to. Including the token.

First question is probably best answered by @tchellomello, why do we cache some data and not just fetch it freshly from Ring API?

If we decide to keep the cache around, we should merge `cache_file` into `token_updater`. The `token_updater` approach is nice, because it removes all caching logic from the library. It just calles a method and it's up to the consumer of the lib to decide how to store it.

Some nice discoveries:

- Comparing **very deeply** nested (List[List[List[x]]] or more) fails because graphene.List() allocations differ in memory for every subtype generated. But from a GraphQL type validation standpoint, it makes no sense to compare memory allocations like that. So I switched our type comparisons to `str(type) == str(expected_type)` and we are now able to test these (very much edge) cases. 
- Together with the stuff mentioned above, I also devised a simple way to have a loop compare those properties, but also have a sane way to retrieve the failed property (so that we don't lose any kind of dev experience), so that the failed properties are very easy to spot (Screenshot provided below - I think it looks nice).

```py
def test_sqlalchemy_hybrid_property_type_inference():
    class ShoppingCartItemType(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
        class Meta:
            model = ShoppingCartItem
            interfaces = (Node,)

    class ShoppingCartType(SQLAlchemyObjectType):
        class Meta:
            model = ShoppingCart
            interfaces = (Node,)

    hybrid_prop_expected_types: Dict[str, Union[Scalar, Structure]] = {
        # Basic types
        "hybrid_prop_str": String,
        "hybrid_prop_int": Int,
        "hybrid_prop_float": Float,
        "hybrid_prop_bool": Boolean,
        "hybrid_prop_decimal": String,  # Decimals should be serialized Strings
        "hybrid_prop_date": Date,
        "hybrid_prop_time": Time,
        "hybrid_prop_datetime": DateTime,
        # Lists and Nested Lists
        "hybrid_prop_list_int": List(Int),
        "hybrid_prop_list_date": List(Date),
        "hybrid_prop_nested_list_int": List(List(Int)),
        "hybrid_prop_first_shopping_cart_item": ShoppingCartItemType,
        "hybrid_prop_shopping_cart_item_list": List(Int)
    }

    assert sorted(list(ShoppingCartType._meta.fields.keys())) == sorted([
        # Columns
        "id",
        # Append Hybrid Properties from Above
        *hybrid_prop_expected_types.keys()
    ])

    for hybrid_prop_name, hybrid_prop_expected_return_type in hybrid_prop_expected_types.items():
        hybrid_prop_field = ShoppingCartType._meta.fields[hybrid_prop_name]

        # this is a simple way of showing the failed property name
        # instead of having to unroll the loop.
        assert (hybrid_prop_name, str(hybrid_prop_field.type)) \
               == (hybrid_prop_name, str(hybrid_prop_expected_return_type))
        assert hybrid_prop_field.description is None  # "doc" is ignored by hybrid property
```

Hi @mthrok. I don't think there's any Python involved in this part of the code. The `_set_vectors_states ` states function always creates a tuple of size 4 when a `vector` is serialized, and the `_get_vectors_from_states ` always expects 4 states during deserialization. `std::tuple_size<decltype(states)>` actually allows us to detect the number of items inside of states before deserializing. The reason this is done is for backward compatibility since in the future we may change the number of items in the tuple that we serialize in the `states` tuple. If the number of items has indeed changed, then we can return an error message to the user so they understand why they can't deserialize their serialized vector.

Finally figured out what's going on:

When the bootstrap script is installed with `pip install -e` (as opposed to a plain `pip install`), the directory `west/src` ends up in `sys.path`, since the `bootstrap` package happens to be in there.

That indirectly makes `west/src/west` importable, provided it is a module, which is how the tests found them.

IOW, `pytest` previously relied on having run `pip install -e .` first.

Few comments:
1) `slice` and `range` are two different things. `slice` is a built-in [function](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#slice) that returns a slice object. `range` is a [type](https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#func-range).
2) `list`, `tuple`, and `range` are collectively known as [basic sequence types](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#typesseq).
3) We support a range-based string using a dash or a colon. e.g. `session.channels['1-4']` and `session.channels['1:4']`. These are just strings as far as the language is concerned. But we have [logic](https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/blob/f074be747bf32054fce5ffe13047a9412806030b/generated/nifake/nifake/_converters.py#L66) to handle these.

We can be explicit about the types we support, along with an example for each:
- `int`
  - `session.foo[42]`
- `str`
  - Single instance: `session.foo['42']`
  - A range of instances: `session.foo['0-2']` or `session.foo['0:2']`
- `list`
  - `session.foo[0,1,2]`
- `tuple`
  - `session.foo[(0,1,2)]`
- `range`
  - `session.foo[range(10)]`
- `slice`
  - `session.foo[range(10)[3:5]]`

Going off what @MisRob said, a public method on `EmbeddedSidePanel` to focus it on the appropriate internal element makes sense to me. 

There's a pattern we can follow in plain HTML-- you can think of `<input>` as just another Vue component, so when you want to focus on it, you call that method `.focus()`. That allows the component to decide how to handle that appropriately.

The other case is when the element or Vue component wraps other elements or components, like the `FullScreenSidePanel`. In this instance, you want it to trap the focus, so implementing `FocusTrap` would either be inside this component or in whatever component defines the wrapped content, since that component has access to its children. In this case, I think the pattern is to use events. So `FullScreenSidePanel` could emit and event like `@shouldFocus` to trigger focusing on the appropriate element.

Although, the current `FocusTrap` implementation relies on access to the component children, which conflicts with the use case of a wrapper component. To make it work with this approach, it could also emit events when the first and last elements should be focused and avoid requiring the elements as props.

So as an example, it could look like this:
```html
<FullScreenSidePanel @shouldFocus="$refs.embedded.focus()">
  <EmbeddedSidePanel ref="embedded" />
</FullScreenSidePanel>
```
where `$emit("shouldFocus")` occurs in the `$nextTick` on `mount()` and on `<FocusTrap @focusFirstEl="$emit('shouldFocus')">` if it was modified to emit events.

Hmm yeah good point, "Basic" was supposed to connote both the simplicity of the data processing operations and the size of the data, but I think it only does the former. I'm still not sure what the best example groupings are.

Here are some example group ideas:
- Small-scale data processing - this should be simple data processing (one-liner transforms and basic aggregations) on small data
  - I'm focusing on these right now for tabular, text, and imagery data
- Large-scale data processing -  this should be simple data processing (one-liner transforms and basic aggregations) on large data and a multi-node cluster
  - Our large-scale ML ingest example is ~close to this, although it's very light on the data processing (it's just read + shuffle)
  - Do we want to delineate examples based on data/cluster scale?
- Simple/basic data processing - this should be simple data processing operations (one-liner transforms and basic aggregations)
  - Not sure if this is a good category; are we going to have a "complex data processing" group? I don't think so, since those "complex" examples will be tailored to use cases, so framing it as "complex" probably isn't right.
- End-to-end ML pipelines - this would be full ML ingest and/or batch inference example pipelines, with real preprocessing
  - These would be closer to the Ray Train examples, with a bit more preprocessing involved: https://docs.ray.io/en/master/train/examples.html#ray-datasets-integration-examples

If you write only (for example a property setter), but the device responds with some confirmation, the read_buffer remains filled with that confirmation. If you now try to write again (setting something or as part of `ask`), the exception will be raised, because the next reading will (with the real instrument) return that confirmation and not, what you expected.

In order to fix it, you have to read the buffer, either with "read" itself, or with the property's check_set_errors which does read.

The primary thing we're testing here is that the python package works, with the new integrated-opencl build, whether or not there is an OpenCL platform present at runtime.  So I've changed the test_windows.ps1 script to run test_dual.py twice (for Windows, bdist builds only): once before OpenCL is installed in the CI VM, and once after it is installed.  The environment variable LIGHTGBM_TEST_DUAL_CPU_GPU is first set to "1" to indicate that there is no OpenCL, and then reset to "2" to indicate that OpenCL is present.  The expected behavior is tested for in test_dual.py (see the new comments in that file for details).  For all other builds, LIGHTGBM_TEST_DUAL_CPU_GPU is not set at all and both tests in test_dual.py are skipped.

> Target be restructured to leverage more of the language we already have rule authors use?

Certainly possible (with some substantial redesign), but this goes against the design goal of a Field-centric API where all the logic lives in Fields and Targets simply pick-and-choose which Fields they want to use.

Contrived, but plausible, example in a world without default globs:

```python
class PythonSources(Sources):
  required = True
  
  def validate_snapshot(snapshot):
     # code to validate only `.py` files

class PythonLibrary(Target):
  alias = "python_library"
  core_fields = (PythonSources, Compatibility, ...)

class PythonTests(Target):
  alias = "python_tests"
  core_fields = (PythonSources, Compatibility, ...)
```

vs.

```python
class PythonSources(Sources): 
  def validate_snapshot(snapshot):
     # code to validate only `.py` files

class PythonLibrary(Target):
  alias = "python_library"
  sources: PythonSources
  compatibility: Optional[Compatiblility]
  ...

class PythonTests(Target):
  alias = "python_tests"
  sources: PythonSources
  compatibility: Optional[Compatiblility]
  ...
```

It's a design goal for a `Target` definition to be no more than 3 lines of code, which means we put logic like "This field is required" in the field itself rather than the target.

--

Likewise, if we make `Fields` be optional on Targets, then we now break the design for most of the core abstractions like `tgt.get(Field)`. 

```python
class Files(Target):
   required_fields = (FilesSources,)
   optional_fields = (Tags,)
```

Now, we can't have MyPy know that `tgt[FilesSources]` returns `FilesSources` rather than `Optional[FilesSources]` because that is runtime information that the field happens to be included in `Files.required_fields`. At the same time, we would need to ensure that `tgt[Tags]` is inferred as `Optional[Tags]`, and then handle the `Optional` in the call site, which is clunky.

--

> If not, the smell here is an explosion of Field variant types. You now need a subclass for requiredness, then another for ?

We already have an explosion of `Fields`. This is the natural consequence of typed fields. For example, we already have `PythonBinarySources`, `PythonLibrarySources`, and `PythonTestsSources`, because they each behave differently.

In writing bindings for the core target types like `Resources` the past two days, I've found that (at least for those bindings), using `required` will only result in one additional Field definition I would have typically avoided: using `ResourcesSources` instead of the parent class `Sources`.

Before we get too deep with this review, I just want to clarify a few things about the context behind this change. This is motivated because you want support for periodic transmissions where the CAN message data can be arbitrarily modified, right?

First of all, do any interfaces support arbitrary modifications when doing periodic transmissions natively, or will every interface need to use a userspace implementation? I'm not arguing that there's no desire for this feature—there's clearly a desire for support, since this comes up every few months whenever someone uses the `send_periodic` API. But as `python-can` primarily is providing an API that abstracts over CAN interfaces, if no interface supports it, then all we're doing is providing a `Thread` or scheduling mechanism that can be implemented by any user in their own application.

Also, I'm curious why you chose to go about this by making `data` a generator as opposed to introducing a new API that takes a `Message` generator. This means that this wouldn't support a use case where someone wants messages sent ever `x` seconds where the Arbitration ID _and_ the data can vary between Messages (instead of just modifying the data), which seems like a valid use case if we're already supporting arbitrary modifications of the data when doing periodic transmissions. Was there a reason why you decided to implement it this way?

> At present we may be in a position where if we detect this situation we need to simply crash the client.

Yeah, I think this is the best path, at least in the short term. If the user asserts something that trinity finds to be false, we should crash.

That means we should be extra careful to help people not pick a checkpoint that gets uncled (it was already true, just a reminder).

One thing to note: it gives other clients a way to crash our client, if they know where our checkpoint is: they respond with an uncle at the last unknown header in the gap. So one way to avoid crashing hard is to treat the very last header as invalid if it's not a parent of the canonical block at the child's slot. We would have to print loud warnings, though. But having a client crash that is externally triggerable is a problem.

```python
def parse_member_assignment(buf):
    '''Parse Kafka group assignment'''
    import struct
    assigns = []
    if not buf:
        return assigns
    index = struct.calcsize('>hi')
    _version, assign_len = struct.unpack('>hi', buf[0:index])
    for _ in range(assign_len):
        size = struct.calcsize('>h')
        str_len = struct.unpack('>h', buf[index:index + size])[0]
        index += size
        topic = buf[index: index + str_len].decode('utf-8')
        index += str_len;
        size = struct.calcsize('>i')
        part_len = struct.unpack('>i', buf[index:index + size])[0]
        index += size
        parts = []
        for _ in range(part_len):
            part = struct.unpack('>i', buf[index:index + size])[0]
            parts.append(part)
            index += size
        assigns.append((topic, parts))
    return assigns
```

Thanks @simonlingoogle  for your input, but I don't agree with you on this  and feel strongly (and know from my experience) that _"relaxing the requirement for the `otSrpClientService` to be immutable after ownership is passed to OT"_ is not a good idea and it is error-prone (can lead to tricky bugs and incorrect API use).

> User should already be aware that OT APIs should be called in the OT thread. 

The `otSrpClientServcie` is not an API. It is an object which we expect the caller to allocate it, populate it, and then pass it to OT API. It can be created/populated in another OS thread/task (any OS context). There is no reason to restrict how it is being created by caller in the first place (in general sense) and limit it to same OS thread/task as OT. Once it is given to OT, it is owned by OT and becomes immutable.

> Furthermore, we can make it clear in the doxygen of otSrpServiceCommitService interface.

It is much simpler and easier for developers to understand and follow a rule that once ownership is given to OT the `otSrpClientServcie` become immutable (must not be changed by caller anymore). The ownership model is commonly used in multi-threaded (multi-task) designs.

Compared to something like this:
- `otSrpClientServcie` can be allocated and updated anyway the user want (in any thread/task OS context) before it is passed to OT APIs. 
- But after it is passed to OT APIs, it can be changed/updated only from same OS context where the OT API are being called.

This is complex to explain and hard to follow for users (easy to forget and use incorrectly).

> The problem is that we are introducing an API DeleteService, which however is NOT meant to be used to delete a service.

The `DeleteServcie()` is only meant for deleting a service (without notifying the server) and claiming back ownership of `otSrpClientServcie` immediately.

The caller can choose to use it to update a service by deleting it, claiming back the entry and then adding it back sameway that `RemoveServcie()` can be used today for this.

> If we want to provide an API for updating an existing service, I think we should provide an API just for that, and make it clear how user can update the service data. We have already decided in our original design that it's callers' responsibility to manage the otSrpClientService buffers, so it should be fine if we continue to follow this design?

We don't want to provide an API for updating an existing servcie, because it relaxes the notion of ownership of service entries.

No, `contract-caller` would be `C_A`, since that's the most recent caller of `contract-call!` --

`contract-caller` is the "head" of the `contract-call!` stack.

```
;; CONTRACT C_B
(define-public (foo)
   (begin (print tx-sender)
              (print contract-caller)
              (ok 1)))

;; CONTRACT C_A
(define-public (foo)
   (begin (print tx-sender)
              (print contract-caller)
              ;; while invoking contract-call, the contract-caller is set to C_A (the current contract)
              (contract-call! c_b foo) 
              (as-contract 
                 ;; tx-sender and contract-caller both updated to C_A in the
                 ;;     evaluation of as-contracts arg.
                 (begin (print tx-sender) (print contract-caller)
                 ;; while invoking contract-call, the contract-caller is set to C_A (the current contract)
                 ;;   but, in this context it was _already_ C_A.
                            (contract-call! c_b foo)))))
```

This is for determining the overall `ptr_lib` from a tree structure of nested array nodes. I split the recursive part and the `ptr_lib`-determining part, with the recursion here in `_util.py` as a utility function. That way, the logic of how to walk down a tree of nested array nodes is in one place, and if we ever add a new node type, it can be updated in one place.

To do that, the action of finding `ptr_lib` has to be defined in a function, with that function being passed in here. The `braodcast_and_apply` and `apply_recursively` work the same way. This is an example of the value of functional programming. There are old articles about how LISP seemed "magical" to C programmers back in the 80's—this kind of factorization over arbitrary functions is very useful! (Though if it's overused, it leads to Javascript's callback hell.)

When defining a function the author needs to import the domain and version which they are using for defining the function body. For example if the function body contains op 'A' from domain 'X' then the author should import {'X', 'then_latest_ version'} in the function body. This way any model which imports version equal to or greater than the one imported by the function proto should work. If the schema for op 'A' changes then the function op needs to be updated with the new schema for op 'A' and corresponding import.

I forgot to carry over this comment from #555 (in part because I forgot the details - this goes to show you how scary it really is!). I'm not sure if we ever debated the costs/benefits of this design trade-off.

```
I have edited the plan to defer all replacement of auto-escaped chars to their original form until python post processing. The reason why is that in addition to convering <,>,& to <, etc. there is also a whole host of special characters are converted to entity numbers, e.g. &#0xHEX; and it would be best to capture those with one single regex - easier done in python than in mssql. A bit more info is here https://www.w3schools.com/html/html_entities.asp

An important design alternative here is that it is possible to avoid the auto-escaping if we use XML PATH(''), TYPE).value('.', 'VARCHAR(max)') - the only downside is that this form isn't robust to inputs containing certain unicode characters and would need to be both preprocessed to pass through the TYPE function and postprocessed to undo that preprocessing. b/c it is robust to all character inputs (to my knowledge), the method using just XML PATH('') only needs postprocessing.
```

There is a background story! 

This is mostly about distributed training. Let's say we have a Ray Train trainer that starts 2 distributed workers a 4 CPUs, so 8 CPUs in total. Ever since we've been using Ray Tune as the execution backend, this requires us to start a remote "head" worker (comparable to the RLlib learner) that starts these 2 distributed workers. But because the head worker is also a remote actor, it requires resources.

So now we're in a situation where we actually would need 9 CPUs (1 for the trainable actor, 2*4 for the distributed workers). That's undesirable, as it means we need bigger clusters (especially when tuning). Also (unlike the rllib trainer), the trainable worker is not doing much at all, it just does a bit of coordination between the distributed workers.

So the solution is to pass `num_cpus=0` to this actor. 

So now we need to somehow specify resources (a PlacementGroupFactory) that schedules the first actor with 0 CPUs. So what we want is `[{"CPU": 0}, {"CPU": 1}, {"CPU": 1}]`.

At first, we wanted to make this work without any special casing. Placement groups however don't accept empty bundles. The first workaround was to pass `[{"CPU": 0.01}, {"CPU": 0.99}, {"CPU": 1}]` or so which is obviously not great from a user perspective. The second workaround is what we're currently doing: We don't schedule the first "bundle" but just schedule the first actor onto the first bundle with empty resources.

I agree this is not ideal - e.g. what happens if we pass another empty bundle in one of the workers? At the moment this will just raise an error. 

The current solution works well from a user perspective, though. So I'd say let's keep it for now until we feel the need to come up with something better.

> It appears we are having an issue with the `_sha` method when referring to a PR.

This is a problem with the way we manage our ref space actually that probably requires more discussion between us. In my first try, this line:

```
            sha = _sha(project, '{qual_manifest_rev_branch}')
```

Was this:

```
            sha = _sha(project, project.revision)
```

But the problem was that this didn't work if the revision was a branch (which is exactly what it *must* do and the main reason for its existence is), because the current fetching code doesn't pull in the remote's head ref space (into `refs/west/*`) unless the revision is a SHA! :laughing: 

So my first response was to change the fetching code to just unconditionally always fetch the entire remote project ref space and try to freeze using that. But I thought this would be a more controversial change and that I could just get away with using manifest-rev for now. I see now that I was wrong, because west does not have a manifest-rev branch.

So our two options are:

1. Add a manifest-rev branch to west and use that (I actually kind of like that for consistency)
2. Fetch the entire remote project's ref space always in all cases and use that (I think this has its merits too)

Since this is your area I wonder if you have a preference?

- All public APIs are subject to semver style deprecation policy, meaning no breaking changes wiithout bumping the major version number, and breaking changes **should** always go through a formal deprecation cycle and raise proper warnings before being changed.

we're passing API level objects to backends when we're doing groupby on a list of Series whose parent is not grouping frame
```python
df = pd.DataFrame({"a": np.arange(3)})

s1, s2 = pd.Series(np.arange(3)), pd.Series(np.arange(3, 6))
df.groupby([s1, s2])
```

In that case at `df.groupby(...)` we will hit [this](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/dd691cd0d4bb5403b38242563ed8b6ddb316734a/modin/pandas/dataframe.py#L403-L420) if-else branch and will pass pure "by" with API-level Series to GroupByObject. 

GroupByObject constructor [do not](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/dd691cd0d4bb5403b38242563ed8b6ddb316734a/modin/pandas/groupby.py#L43-L92) convert high level objects to query compilers, and `_wrap_aggregation` method also [don't do](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/blob/dd691cd0d4bb5403b38242563ed8b6ddb316734a/modin/pandas/groupby.py#L786-L795) that.

> ... just ask the user to try again ...

The trouble is that the error is happening while we're handling a usage error to begin with.
So we don't really want them to try the exact same operation again -- and even if they did, we'd get new and different tokens back.

We *could* put another blob into the token storage DB, but that's dedicating much more effort to this scenario than I think is appropriate.

It's not necessarily a problem if the CLI hard-errors in this case on failing revocation. The user would get a slightly surprising error message, but maybe that's acceptable.

Perhaps the best course of action is to use a `try-finally` construct (no `except` block) and put the error message echo in the `finally`. That way, we're guaranteed to show our desired message, even if the revocation fails. And revocation failure would show the underlying error.

I think we have to worry about races with this conditional, but not with the for-loop itself.

1) There is a race between a batch going from ACCEPTING_MESSAGES to STARTING in commit() and this check for the state being STARTING.
2) If a batch is in the state SUCCESS or ERROR, this for-loop wont run. So, wait() may return before the futures return with a result.

It would be good to take the lock to prevent these races.

How about:

should_wait = false
with self._state_lock:
            if self._status != base.BatchStatus.ACCEPTING_MESSAGES:
                    should_wait = true
if should_wait:
        for future in self._futures:
                future.result()

I would also worry about races in modifications to self._futures. However, in the way this class is set up, reads of self._futures only take place after all writes are done (aka, the class is no longer in state ACCEPTING_MESSAGES). The code I gave above maintains this invariant. This is not obvious. I wish we could document it.

If a contour is crossed by a straight line, then there may be two intersection points.
These two points are then added to one of two lists: 
 - list of points with higher X (for coronal plane) or Y (for sagittal plane)
 - list of points with lower X (for coronal plane) or Y (for sagittal plane)
 
 One list will form "upper part" of the resulting contour, another - "lower part". 
Finally we merge "upper part" with "lower part", but to maintain correct sequence of point we go though lower part in reverse way.

This is all there to prevent us from sending messages to a room with offline or no users, which would lead to expiring transfers (I think?).

So... still not blocking, but: one more wildcard thought here (cc @Eric-Arellano). The desire to add more per-node information draw me back to comparison to `peek`

What if we:
1. added computation of the `dependencies` of a target to `peek` (...optionally?)
2. _did not add_ the `--output` mode to `dependencies`
2. _removed_ the `--output` mode from `dependees`

Conceptually, this would mean that the `dependencies` and `dependees` goals were always about rendering flat lists of addresses, and `peek` was always about rendering per-node information in the graph. Assuming the above changes, the way to get information about every node in the graph (including about its dependencies) would just be:
```console
./pants peek ::
```
To get information about only the dependencies of a particular target would be:
```
./pants dependencies --transitive $target | xargs ./pants peek
```

An open question is whether computing dependencies is too expensive for `peek` to do by default... i.e., whether you'd want a flag like `./pants peek --compute=dependencies ::` (leaving room for a `--compute=sources` for the other `AsyncField` type).

app [Address(lib2/a.py), Address(lib2/b.py), Address(lib1)]
lib1 []
lib2/a.py []
lib2/a.py []

Hi Felix, I think it is best to explain it this way: 

If there is no prefix, we add first_step 1 time
If there is a prefix with length 1, we add first step 2 times
If there is a prefix with length 2, we add first step 3 times
If there is a prefix with length 3, we add first step 4 times
...

So the number of times we add first_step is equal to the length of prefix + 1. Rolling to the right reflects this.

> Then I assume the right thing to do in the future when requesting all possible Zephyr modules, is to do:

Not quite. Remember that `west.manifest.Manifest.from_file()` will be the deprecated version that works the same way as it does today, with a `ManifestProject`. The new ways to get a manifest without a `ManifestProject` will be `west.manifest.from_file()` or `west.manifest.from_topdir()`.

Some of the details for zephyr modules are still TBD in my mind. I think one way to handle this cleanly would be to introduce a new `Repository` class, like this:

```py
class Repository:
    '''Attributes:

    - path: relative to topdir
    - abspath: absolute, host form
    - posixpath: absolute, POSIX form
    '''

    def git():
        # run git in the repository

class Project(Repository):
    '''A repository that is also a project in a manifest file,
    but has other attributes as well, like 'url', that Repository
    does not have.'''

class Manifest:
    '''New attribute:

    - repositories: List[Repository], includes all projects
      and the manifest repository
    '''

```

Then in zephyr you could do:

```py
import west.manifest

manifest = west.manifest.from_topdir()
modules = [repository for repository in manifest.repositories if is_zephyr_module(repository)]
for module in modules:
    handle_module(module)
```

Then we could probably keep get_projects() around for backwards compatibility, but return an error unless `legacy_manifest_projects` was `True` at init time. I think the list comprehension style is a little cleaner anyway.

I think putting the `_VarVal` container in place is the easiest way to do the following:

1- quickly error if assigning to undeclared variable

2- quickly error if reading from unassigned variable

3- manage scope / lifetime of value correctly

Imagine the mechanics of getting this right:

`(Let(a=Var()), Let(a=1), Let(a=2))`

So, first you put `a=Var()` in the spec.

Then `Let(a=1)`, so you backtrack up and see the `Var()`.  And then... what?

If you replace the `Var()` with 1, now `Let(a=2)` will not see the `Var()` when it backtracks.

So you need to durably record the allocation somehow.

It's probably quite simple, you just need to call `cythonize()` with the list of files that you want to compile:
https://docs.cython.org/en/latest/src/userguide/source_files_and_compilation.html#basic-setup-py
but only on CPython, e.g. this is what lxml does for its .py files
https://github.com/lxml/lxml/blob/dcab10594a2a3bec2f8302f68205dd0204c21c65/setupinfo.py#L19-L31

Whether that makes it (substantially) faster depends on your code. I think I remember that we discussed this back at the time, right?

The ElementPath implementation in ElementTree and lxml is quite fast in Python because it prepares as much as possible ahead of time and then uses a very efficient chain of tiny generators to select nodes from the tree. Compiling that with Cython gives another notable boost, because it drops those generator implementations down into C.

Your critical module seems to be `xpath_token.py`. I'd try compiling only that, and then profile the compiled version and tune it into something that runs fast when compiled (allowing for performance regressions in interpreted CPython). Users who care about performance can then use a binary wheel with CPython, while PyPy will probably still make something fast out of the tuned Python code. Typical changes could be: reorder type checks to test for builtin types before more complex user types, avoid method/function calls and do a bit of inlining instead, maybe convert `XPathToken` (why is that a `MutableSequence`?) and (some of) its subclasses to extension types (`@cython.cclass`) and use type annotations to avoid Python method calls. Not sure if the latter is doable in the current design, though.

This might look confusing but syntactically it's not assigning a variable to itself; it's defining a property on the element and assigning its value to be the imported module from `import * as tf_feature_flags from './feature-flags';`

You can see how this mechanism works a bit better with the existing tf_globals code:

1) We create a `tf-globals` custom element that basically just holds a `tf_globals` property that represents the symbols from the Polymer static utilities module: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/components/tf_globals/globals-polymer.ts

2) We define a type for this property so that the TypeScript code on the Angular side can access it in a typesafe manner: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/webapp/tb_polymer_interop_types.ts;l=30-32;drc=046667d00df2316eefcda9a4ef058e83221bad77

3) In the Angular code where we actually need to call the `tf_globals` static utilities, we create a custom element, then access its `tf_globals` property: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/webapp/deeplink/hash.ts;l=30-35;drc=367839f30de3968eff5fecb8b38e1d5782434b6b

> ceil mode is specific to Pytorch where SAME is to TF.

Yes, initially there is only auto_pad in ONNX pooling family operators and later ONNX brought ceil_mode from PyTorch. When introducing ceil_mode, the combination behavior of these two attributes (auto_pad+ceil_mode) are not specified and caused confusion: https://github.com/onnx/onnx/issues/3314. Since it's an undefined behavior in ONNX-level, I am slightly inclined to make ONNX definition clear as current ONNX Runtime's behavior: honor ceil_mode when using auto_pad, to prevent runtime behavior changes, although in practice there shouldn't any real-world models using them (auto_pad+ceil_mode) simultaneously since one is from PyTorch and another is from TensorFlow. It seems fine to have this combination, as long as the document is clear.

Another option is making ONNX forbid using auto_pad and ceil_mode at the same time, but I am not 100% sure whether it would break backward compatibility in runtime.

def flatten_reverse_dict(d):
    sep = "___"
    [flat_dict] = pd.json_normalize(d, sep=sep).to_dict(orient='records')

> Yes, and so a higher-level component (say workflow server) could act on the failed job by re-scheduling etc. right?

Fault tolerance and error handling topics are complex. Depends. From a higher-level component perspective, yes - but only if that higher-level component knows how to fix the problem and resubmit the job. Take a look at the fault tolerance and idempotence description (coordination project task in the ZenHub board) of the processing worker:

> **Fault tolerance**
To increase fault tolerance, manual acknowledgement mode must be used. In this case,
    if the processing finished successfully, send a positive ACK signal.
    if the processing ended in failure, try again 3 times. If it's still failed, send a negative ACK signal to let the queuing system re-queue the message.
If there is no ACK received (e.g., consumer crash), the queuing system will automatically re-queue the message after timeout. [By default](https://www.rabbitmq.com/consumers.html#acknowledgement-timeout), it's 30 minutes. This value might need to be adjusted to fit best to our use case.
**Idempotence**
If there is something wrong, e.g. network, the message will be sent again to the consumer. Make sure to check the [redeliver](https://www.rabbitmq.com/confirms.html#automatic-requeueing) property to make sure that we don't process a page twice.

According to that description:
1. The processing worker restarts the OCR-D processor with the same parameters 3 times (**Note**: To keep it simple the current implementation runs only once and fails once, not 4 times - is there even a case when we would like to run more than once here? Assuming that failing once will probably also fail 4 times if nothing in the processing message is changed.)
2. The processing worker sending negative ACK (NACK) does not really make sense here if the failed processing message is requeued as it is again without any changes. A failed message will still fail. The only time when it makes sense to send a NACK is when say the processing worker receives a termination signal and catches it (not supported in the current state) - then the processing message can be requeued back. In that case, it is still unknown whether the processing message will potentially fail or not in the future due to external errors, e.g., in the workspace. (**Note**: in either case, the workspace will be modified to a certain extent and we need a way to track this - a backup mechanism or extension of the Mets caching in the core. Of course, with page-level processing this becomes an easier task.)
3. Ideally, the failed processing message should be forwarded to another queue ([dead letter exchange](https://www.rabbitmq.com/dlx.html)) to avoid an infinite loop of requeuing failing messages to the same queue.
4. The redeliver flag/property of the processing message hints that the message was requeued so either of these happened:
- The message was NACKed due to consumer timeout (worker has failed or still processing)
- The message was NACKed manually (the processor has failed 4 times or the worker was terminated and caught the termination signal to NACK the message before termination). 

However, simply by checking that property, we could not really say whether: 
- The message was already processed - don't process it again/twice (as described above)
- The message was not (fully) processed - (re)process it. In that case, we again need to know exactly where to continue from. In case the processing message is a single page, then I guess it's not a big deal to process the entire page again with the `--overwrite` flag.

5. The Workflow Server can check the processing job status in the DB / Result queue. If the processing job has failed, the Workflow Server knows exactly which job failed and can resubmit the job through the Processing Server. Of course, assuming that the Workflow Server is smart enough to be able to fix the error (which is again a broad topic). Potentially, user involvement will be required here to manually submit the job after fixing the external error.

More regarding the workflow execution:
Lately, I have been busy with implementing a Nextflow script to be executed by the Workflow Server that does the following:
1 Sends a processing job to the Processing Server
2 Connects a RabbitMQ consumer to listen to the result queue of the processor specified in the job
3 After consuming a single message the consumer is disconnected 
4 Based on the job state: 
   - Success - the next OCR-D process in the workflow is executed
   - Fail - the workflow is terminated

However, still facing a problem that I was not able to solve yet - synchronization between the blocking consumer and the parent process that executes the Nextflow processes inside the Nextflow workflow. The thing is that I have been also digging in the Nextflow source code to get a better understanding of how processes and threads are utilized since in the documentation itself it's not obvious what is a separate process and what is a separate thread of a specific thread (and honestly, why would they clarify such detail when not relevant for a big portion of the users). What I am planning is to try a polling strategy. Since the workflow cannot continue without having the status of the previous job, this still makes sense.

I am not sure if that would be the elegant approach to running workflows - i.e. with a RabbitMQ consumer inside the Nextflow script. Especially, since the Nextflow scripts have to be submitted by the users. On the other hand, if the Workflow Server creates worker processes for submitting processing jobs and consumers for consuming from result queues - we lose the point of having the Nextflow in the middle between the Workflow Server and the Processing Server. 

Hope this helps to get more insights.

> BTW, for a discussion of lower-level error handling (in the processor itself, i.e. at page level) see https://github.com/OCR-D/core/issues/579. But re-entry of partially run workflows is a cross-over topic.

Figured out long poll. Implemented as the following:
- controller keeps a set of `active_nodes` and have an instance of `LongPollHost`
- during each `update()` call, controller will generate a set of `active_nodes`
- if the new `active_nodes` set changes from the current state, `notify_changed()` will be called
- both http proxy state and http proxy each has an instance of `LongPollClient` listening to the change on the `ACTIVE_NODES` namespace
- http proxy state has a callback to set the `self_draining` flag on itself. And the `update()` call will use this to determine the new state
- http proxy also has a callback to set the `self._draining` flag on itself. And there are logic to log flag changes and if we need to reset the dummy object reference (this is for minimizing `ray.put()` calls only when necessary). The flag will also continue to be used to determine the response of `/-/routes` and `/-/healthz`

There's a lot going on here.

Architecturally, I'd like to see this go into its own class, the way the scatter methods use ReceiveScatterOutput.  I'll call in `LoopStep` for the sake of discussion.

This would let you encapsulate the loop logic into a class instead of adding a large amount of new code to a method that was previously much shorter.

I would also like to see the loop logic go into a function that returns a JobsGenerator, similar to the way scatter works.

So the sketch of the way this would work is:

1. in `try_make_job`, if there's a loop, it calls `jobs = LoopStep().job(step, inputobj, callback, runtimeContext)`  This creates a LoopStep object and calls the job generator method on it.
2. in `LoopStep.job()` it checks the loop condition.
3. LoopStep has a method called loop_callback
4. if the condition passes, it yields the first iteration `step.job(inputobj, self.loop_callback, runtimeContext)`
5. When that finishes, loop_callback is called with the result of the step.  Record the results, update the input object, and set a flag that an iteration has completed
6. Control will periodically come back to `LoopStep.job` which will check that flag.  If there's nothing ready yet, return None
7. If an iteration completed, we go back to the top and check the loop condition.  If the loop condition is still true, yield step.job() with the updated inputobj
8. If the loop condition is false, call the _original_ callback with the final results

Thanks for raising this perspective again, we surely have a tendency to define things at class level, although I would argue that that is primarily because Python's properties, which we use heavily, are a class-, not an instance-level feature.

I recognize and agree with some of your cited advantages
* centralisation of channel instantiation in `Instrument.__init__`
* 2-channel/4-channel variant definition. The current approach could cover this outside of __init__ with a `NR_CHANNELS` class attribute that gets overridden in the 2-channel child class.
* preserved possibility to use `add_child` directly (that's actually neutral as it is present in both alternatives)

I am not sure I understand some points:
* What is the use case for a walkthrough over all "things" without having to instantiate the instruments (and also, by extension, the things themselves)? Why is it important that we are able to do this?
* Why is it better to have channels defined at class level? Where is the distinction important? Actually, only the channel "schema"/makeup (if you will) is defined at class level, as you say the channels themselves are created at instantiation.
* Why is it easier to reuse channels in different instruments? The channels are separately defined classes, irrespective of which scheme we are using to put them into the instrument, no?

I also want to raise some advantages of the currently implemented method to cover point "3" above, over the approach you propose:
* It is easier, via `add_child`'s `prefix` argument with a sensible default, to make clear and encourage/enforce the default naming scheme (i.e. `ch_<id1>`, `ch_<id2>`,...) for the attributes. This will automatically lead to a more homogeneous API across instruments (which we want to see more of in the future, see also #416). If instrument implementors need to define this in a "things" dict, where they have choose their own keys, this will be less automatic, and I suspect it will need more effort during PR review to correct.
* I'm not clear where in the "things" mapping we supply the identifiers of the channel (be it `1`, `2`, or `A`, `B`,...) in the exchanged command strings. This is currently handled in `add_child`'s `id` argument, how do you map the things dict to that? Use the keys? 
* We avoid a class attribute `things` that clutters the already complicated Instrument API, reserves another name, but is useful only during instantiation. Granted, we could delete that in `Instrument.__init__, but still.

Happy to discuss this further to get to a well-rounded design! :-)

I considered making a new helper function where it would be easier to explain what is happening, something like:

```python
def unique(*iterables):
    # unique list of items in order of occurrence
    ordered = []
    # for every item in our iterables, we check to see if it has already been seen,
    # if it has, its added to the list of duplicates, otherwise it's appended to ordered
    duplicates = [
        item
        for item in chain.from_iterable(iterables)
        # shortcircuit for whether to add to duplicates list or to ordered list
        if item in ordered or ordered.append(item)
    ]
    return ordered, duplicates
```

"safer" == "greater probability of the module being available in the unpickling environment and of the object being defined in it (and pertain to it)"

For example, consider a module named `pack.mod`. At loading its state with `load_module()`, we can assume that both Standard Library modules and other modules from the package `pack` are available (otherwise, `pack.mod` itself wouldn't be loadable). So, if at saving its state with `dump_module()`, the unpickleable object `pack.mod.obj` can be saved globally either as `std.lib.obj` or as `pack.other.obj`, should we prefer one over the other? I argue that yes, because the most likely scenario is that the object is defined in the Standard Library module `std.lib` and imported from it in both `pack.mod` and `pack.other`. Plus, there's a small chance that, in the unpickling environment, the module `pack.mod` may exist but `pack.other` may not (for example, if we had slightly different versions of the package). For StdLib packages, however, that's highly unlikely.

That's the logic behind the whole hierarchy. It's an empirical way of maximizing the probability of success of saving by reference in the module's namespace.

This is almost orthogonal to the stratification of self/"builtin"/"user" behavior from that table. The only change is that modules that are part of the same package as the module being saved (if the module is part of any package) are put together with the "builtin" modules even if the package is not appropriately installed. Basically, this hierarchy will define which reference to another module is better, but not if that should be used instead of a reference to the module itself, or if an exception should be raised.

@bmoneke I am resolving this conversation, I think the current implementation is good.

> Its that simple. Targets are really just there to trip you up. 

It would be that simple if we did not allow for a file to have >1 targets, meaning this is legal and even [encouraged in our docs for Py3 migrations](https://www.pantsbuild.org/docs/python-interpreter-compatibility#using-multiple-python-versions-in-the-same-project):

```python
python_tests(name='py2', interpreter_constraints=["==2.7.*"])
python_tests(name='py3', interpreter_constraints=[">=3.6"])
```

> Rather than partitioning by interpreter constraints like we do with Flake8, we would partition by file?

If we do this and only run once-per-file, I realized you'll get confusingly different behavior based on which specs you use:

```bash
./pants fmt path/to/ambiguous.py   # use only one target, e.g. the min interp constraints
./pants fmt path/to/ambiguous.py:py2  # use py2 constraints
./pants fmt path/to/ambiguous.py:py3  # use py3 constraints
```

That's not ideal.

--

So, I think partitioning by file is not realistic with the status quo of interpreter constraints. Meaning, we either give up on autoflake or we do not attempt to robustly handle mixed interpreter constraints. The latter seems far more preferable imo, and users keep asking for autoflake.

@cristianmatache this means keeping what you currently have. We may want to consider changing the default interpreter constraints though to be >=3.5 or even >=3.6...Generally, Pants no longer makes an attempt to support Python 2 Out Of The Box given it's EOL. We want to allow you to configure back in Py2 support, but we would rather optimize for a better experience Out Of The Box with Py3. In this case, by setting the default to 3.5+ or 3.6+, it's more likely that the default config will work with user's repos.

As per my understanding, `conda shell.cmd.exe activate` only returns a single line with a temporary path to the activation script:

```
$ conda shell.cmd.exe activate
/var/folders/1b/z374sygs1s96t_xxxv0hr3lr0000gn/T/tmpot5p01v9.bat                   
```

So this seems to be safe.

---

Full explanation

1. User calls `conda activate "my env"`
2. This enters the `conda` shell command, defined in `conda/shell/condabin/conda.bat`. Since we asked for `activate`, it forwards to `conda/shell/condabin/_conda_activate.bat`, where this PR is adding changes.
3. `_conda_activate.bat` [will call](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/92cfafcd65c338240d562d7a74cf7285f2eb5670/conda/shell/condabin/_conda_activate.bat#L41) `conda shell.cmd.exe <activate|deactivate>`, which takes us to `conda.cli.main`. There, `source`'d commands are [split](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/92cfafcd65c338240d562d7a74cf7285f2eb5670/conda/cli/main.py#L125) from `install`-like commands. 
4. `main_sourced` will delegate to the relevant activator class. Since we have `cmd.exe`, then it calls [`conda.activate.CmdExeActivator.execute()`](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/92cfafcd65c338240d562d7a74cf7285f2eb5670/conda/activate.py#L169).
5. `.execute()` delegates to the relevant subcommand (`activate` in our case), which delegates to `_finalize()` (a method that writes a given string to stdout OR a temporary file and returns its path).
6. This temporary path is printed to STDOUT.
7. We are back to `_conda_activate.bat`, which will now `@CALL` that temporary path.

As a result, we can see that `_finalize()` will only return the temporary path on Windows, so it's a single line, so this method is safe.

It seems like we have a few options for how to report and ultimately present read stats:
1. **(Current)** We can aggregate execution stats from all blocks in this task into a single "task execution stats", that we then report to the stats actor once done reading all blocks.
2. We can send the per-block execution stats to the stats actor, either as the block is read (move `stats_actor.record_task.remote()` into the for loop) or at the end as a list, and then the stats actor can either aggregate these into "task execution stats" or store these as individual block stats. The latter would require some stats actor refactoring to hold a list of block execution stats per read task, but would then give us per-block execution stats granularity.

In terms of stats reporting, I think that (2) with the per-block execution stats stored and reported as per-block execution stats would better match what [`TaskPoolStrategy` is doing](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/0f705888c6fa7106e78b777aff07c72aed639c62/python/ray/data/_internal/compute.py#L411-L423) for e.g. `.map_batches()` tasks, and I think is what we want to do since our stats are block-centric (i.e. "stats for producing this block") rather than task-centric (i.e. "stats for executing this task").

What do y'all think of going with that version of (2), with all block stats reported at the end of the read task, to keep things consistent? We can reexamine this with the upcoming AIR observability overhaul, cc @gjoliver

    def to_publish_app(self):

        This is the relation data that the local app publishes so it is
        visible to all related units. Use this to communicate with related

        Only the leader can set the app-level relation data.

        All values stored in this collection will be automatically JSON
        encoded when they are published. This means that they need to be JSON
        serializable! Mappings stored in this collection will be encoded with
        sorted keys, to ensure that the encoded representation will only change
        if the actual data changes.

        Changes to this data are published at the end of a succesfull hook. The
        data is reset when a hook fails.
        """
        if self._app_data is None:
            # using JSONUnitDataView though it's name includes unit.
            self._app_data = JSONUnitDataView(
                hookenv.relation_get(app=self.application_name,
                                     rid=self.relation_id),
                writeable=True)
        return self._app_data

    @property
    def received_app(self):
        """
        A :class:`~charms.reactive.endpoints.JSONUnitDataView` of the app-level
        data received from this remote unit over the relation, with values
        being automatically decoded as JSON.
        """
        if self._remote_app_data is None:
            # using JSONUnitDataView though it's name includes unit.
            self._remote_app_data = JSONUnitDataView(hookenv.relation_get(
                app=self.application_name,
                rid=self.relation.relation_id))
        return self._remote_app_data

Imho, a token based generator is more resilient towards intermediate list modifications (while still not completely resistent).
- Using the index: Basically _every single element added or removed_ before the current page will cause shifting issues. Specific items in the list will be processed multiple times, which is quite dangerous.
- Using the token: The pagination can break if the exact element used as a token is removed. If any other elements are removed, or elements are added, it's not sure that they are going to be contained in the list.

Imho, the shifting can cause way more damage than a token-based approach.
